From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Jack Thorne (writer)](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne_(writer)&redirect=no "Jack Thorne (writer)"))

British screenwriter and playwright

For other people named Jack Thorne, see [Jack Thorne (disambiguation)](/wiki/Jack_Thorne_(disambiguation) "Jack Thorne (disambiguation)").

| Jack Thorne  [FRSL](/wiki/FRSL "FRSL") | |
| --- | --- |
| Thorne in 2025 | |
| Born | (1978-12-06) 6 December 1978 (age 47)  [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol"), England |
| Alma mater | [Pembroke College, Cambridge](/wiki/Pembroke_College,_Cambridge "Pembroke College, Cambridge") |
| Occupations | * Playwright * screenwriter * producer |

| Spouse | Rachel Mason |

**Jack Thorne** [FRSL](/wiki/Royal_Society_of_Literature "Royal Society of Literature") (born 6 December 1978) is a British playwright, screenwriter, and producer.

Thorne is best known for writing the stage play *[Harry Potter and the Cursed Child](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Cursed_Child "Harry Potter and the Cursed Child")* (2016), the films *[Wonder](/wiki/Wonder_(film) "Wonder (film)")* (2017), *[Enola Holmes](/wiki/Enola_Holmes_(film) "Enola Holmes (film)")* (2020) and the latter's [sequel](/wiki/Enola_Holmes_2 "Enola Holmes 2") (2022), and the television programme [*His Dark Materials*](/wiki/His_Dark_Materials_(TV_series) "His Dark Materials (TV series)") (2019–2022). Between 2010 and 2015, Thorne co-wrote three mini-series – *[This Is England '86](/wiki/This_Is_England_%2786 "This Is England '86")*, *[This Is England '88](/wiki/This_Is_England_%2788 "This Is England '88")* and *[This Is England '90](/wiki/This_Is_England_%2790 "This Is England '90")* – with director [Shane Meadows](/wiki/Shane_Meadows "Shane Meadows").

In 2023, Thorne's four-part mini-series *[Best Interests](/wiki/Best_Interests "Best Interests")* was released starring [Michael Sheen](/wiki/Michael_Sheen "Michael Sheen") and [Sharon Horgan](/wiki/Sharon_Horgan "Sharon Horgan"). It was nominated for several awards, winning Best Limited Series at the [Banff Rockie Awards](/wiki/Banff_World_Media_Festival "Banff World Media Festival").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Thorne's two 2025 mini-series, *[Toxic Town](/wiki/Toxic_Town "Toxic Town")* and *[Adolescence](/wiki/Adolescence_(TV_series) "Adolescence (TV series)")*, were released on [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") to widespread critical acclaim.

## Early life and education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early life and education")]

Thorne was born in [Bristol](/wiki/Bristol "Bristol") on 6 December 1978. He was educated at [St Bartholomew's School](/wiki/St_Bartholomew%27s_School "St Bartholomew's School") in [Newbury, Berkshire](/wiki/Newbury,_Berkshire "Newbury, Berkshire"), and studied politics at [Pembroke College, Cambridge](/wiki/Pembroke_College,_Cambridge "Pembroke College, Cambridge"), matriculating in 1998.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3) He was forced to "degrade" (drop out to return at a later date) due to ill health in his third year, but returned to finish his studies and graduated with lower second-class honours in 2002.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Career")]

### Theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Theatre")]

Thorne's plays for the stage include *When You Cure Me* ([Bush Theatre](/wiki/Bush_Theatre "Bush Theatre") 2005[[5]](#cite_note-5)), *Fanny and Faggot* ([Edinburgh Festival Fringe](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival_Fringe "Edinburgh Festival Fringe") 2004,[[6]](#cite_note-6) [Finborough Theatre](/wiki/Finborough_Theatre "Finborough Theatre") and tour, 2007[[7]](#cite_note-7)), *Stacy* ([Arcola Theatre](/wiki/Arcola_Theatre "Arcola Theatre") and [Trafalgar Studios](/wiki/Trafalgar_Studios "Trafalgar Studios"), 2007[[8]](#cite_note-8)), *Burying Your Brother in the Pavement* ([Royal National Theatre](/wiki/Royal_National_Theatre "Royal National Theatre") Connections Festival 2008[[9]](#cite_note-9)), *2 May 1997* ([Bush Theatre](/wiki/Bush_Theatre "Bush Theatre") 2009[[10]](#cite_note-10)), *Bunny* (Underbelly and tour 2010[[11]](#cite_note-11)) which won a Fringe First at the 2010 [Edinburgh Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_Festival "Edinburgh Festival")[[12]](#cite_note-12) and *Hope* ([Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre"), 2014). He also collaborated on *[Greenland](/wiki/Greenland_(2011_play) "Greenland (2011 play)")* (2011) with [Moira Buffini](/wiki/Moira_Buffini "Moira Buffini"), [Penelope Skinner](/wiki/Penelope_Skinner "Penelope Skinner") and [Matt Charman](/wiki/Matt_Charman "Matt Charman") at the National Theatre. In 2011 he participated in the Bush Theatre's project *[Sixty-Six Books](/wiki/Sixty-Six_Books "Sixty-Six Books")*, for which he wrote a piece based upon a book of the [King James Bible](/wiki/King_James_Bible "King James Bible").[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2012 his version of [Friedrich Duerrenmatt](/wiki/Friedrich_Duerrenmatt "Friedrich Duerrenmatt")'s *[The Physicists](/wiki/The_Physicists "The Physicists")* was staged at the [Donmar Warehouse](/wiki/Donmar_Warehouse "Donmar Warehouse").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

Thorne's 2013 adaptation of the book and film *[Let The Right One In](/wiki/Let_the_Right_One_In_(novel) "Let the Right One In (novel)")* was staged in a production by the [National Theatre of Scotland](/wiki/National_Theatre_of_Scotland "National Theatre of Scotland") at [Dundee Rep Theatre](/wiki/Dundee_Rep_Theatre "Dundee Rep Theatre"), London's [Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre"), West End and New York's [St. Ann's Warehouse](/wiki/St._Ann%27s_Warehouse "St. Ann's Warehouse"). In summer 2015, his play *The Solid Life of Sugar Water* premiered at the Edinburgh Festival Fringe, produced by Graeae Theatre Company and Theatre Royal Plymouth, it then toured in early 2016, with a run at the National Theatre in March 2016.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Together with the composer [Stephen Warbeck](/wiki/Stephen_Warbeck "Stephen Warbeck"), Thorne wrote *Junkyard*, a coming-of-age musical centred around 'The Vench', an [adventure playground](/wiki/Adventure_playground "Adventure playground") in Lockleaze, Bristol.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Thorne wrote the stage play *[Harry Potter and the Cursed Child](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Cursed_Child "Harry Potter and the Cursed Child")*, based on an original story by Thorne, [J. K. Rowling](/wiki/J._K._Rowling "J. K. Rowling") and [John Tiffany](/wiki/John_Tiffany "John Tiffany"), which is running at the [Palace Theatre](/wiki/Palace_Theatre,_London "Palace Theatre, London") in London's West End since August 2016, on [Broadway](/wiki/Broadway_theatre "Broadway theatre") at the [Lyric Theatre](/wiki/Lyric_Theatre_(New_York_City,_1998) "Lyric Theatre (New York City, 1998)") since April 2018, in Melbourne's [Princess Theatre](/wiki/Princess_Theatre_(Melbourne) "Princess Theatre (Melbourne)") since February 2019 and San Francisco's [Curran Theatre](/wiki/Curran_Theatre "Curran Theatre") since December 2019. Thorne also wrote a new adaptation of *[Woyzeck](/wiki/Woyzeck "Woyzeck")* by [Georg Büchner](/wiki/Georg_B%C3%BCchner "Georg Büchner") for the [Old Vic](/wiki/Old_Vic_Theatre "Old Vic Theatre") in 2017 with [John Boyega](/wiki/John_Boyega "John Boyega") in the title role.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] He wrote a new adaptation of *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol_(2017_play) "A Christmas Carol (2017 play)")* by [Charles Dickens](/wiki/Charles_Dickens "Charles Dickens") for the Old Vic for the Christmas 2017 season, directed by [Matthew Warchus](/wiki/Matthew_Warchus "Matthew Warchus"), which has subsequently returned every year, as well as for the 2019 season on Broadway at the [Lyceum Theatre](/wiki/Lyceum_Theatre_(Broadway) "Lyceum Theatre (Broadway)") and the 2020 live broadcast through Old Vic: On Camera due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). In November 2025, *[A Christmas Carol](/wiki/A_Christmas_Carol_(2017_play) "A Christmas Carol (2017 play)")* returned to New York for an Off-Broadway run at the [Perelman Performing Arts Center](/wiki/Perelman_Performing_Arts_Center "Perelman Performing Arts Center").[[17]](#cite_note-17) Thorne rewrote the musical adaptation of *[King Kong](/wiki/King_Kong_(2013_musical) "King Kong (2013 musical)")* for its 2018 Broadway debut.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Thorne penned the play *the end of history* for [Royal Court Theatre](/wiki/Royal_Court_Theatre "Royal Court Theatre") in 2019, starring [David Morrissey](/wiki/David_Morrissey "David Morrissey") and [Lesley Sharp](/wiki/Lesley_Sharp "Lesley Sharp"). Thorne's play *Sunday* premiered at [Atlantic Theatre Company](/wiki/Atlantic_Theatre_Company "Atlantic Theatre Company") in New York in 2019, directed by [Lee Sunday Evans](/wiki/Lee_Sunday_Evans "Lee Sunday Evans"). In June 2021, his adaptation of [*After Life*](/wiki/After_Life_(play) "After Life (play)") based on the [film of the same name](/wiki/After_Life_(film) "After Life (film)") opened at the [National Theatre, London](/wiki/National_Theatre,_London "National Theatre, London").

In April 2023, Thorne's play *[The Motive and the Cue](/wiki/The_Motive_and_the_Cue "The Motive and the Cue")*, directed by [Sam Mendes](/wiki/Sam_Mendes "Sam Mendes"), about the making of [Richard Burton](/wiki/Richard_Burton "Richard Burton") and [John Gielgud](/wiki/John_Gielgud "John Gielgud")'s *[Hamlet](/wiki/Hamlet "Hamlet")*, opened in the Lyttleton Theatre at the [National Theatre](/wiki/Royal_National_Theatre "Royal National Theatre"), before transferring to the [Noël Coward Theatre](/wiki/No%C3%ABl_Coward_Theatre "Noël Coward Theatre") in the [West End](/wiki/West_End_theatre "West End theatre") in December 2023. In June 2023, his play *[When Winston Went to War with the Wireless](/wiki/When_Winston_Went_to_War_with_the_Wireless "When Winston Went to War with the Wireless")*, directed by Katy Rudd, about the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC") during the [1926 General Strike](/wiki/1926_General_Strike "1926 General Strike"), premiered at the [Donmar Warehouse](/wiki/Donmar_Warehouse "Donmar Warehouse"). In November 2023, *[Stranger Things: The First Shadow](/wiki/Stranger_Things:_The_First_Shadow "Stranger Things: The First Shadow")*, a prequel to the [Netflix Series](/wiki/Stranger_Things "Stranger Things") by [Kate Trefry](/wiki/Kate_Trefry "Kate Trefry") with a story by Thorne, Trefry and [The Duffer Brothers](/wiki/The_Duffer_Brothers "The Duffer Brothers") and directed by [Stephen Daldry](/wiki/Stephen_Daldry "Stephen Daldry"), opened at the [Phoenix Theatre, London](/wiki/Phoenix_Theatre,_London "Phoenix Theatre, London").

Thorne's plays are published by [Nick Hern Books](/wiki/Nick_Hern_Books "Nick Hern Books").[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Television

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Television")]

Thorne has written for the TV shows *[Skins](/wiki/Skins_(TV_series) "Skins (TV series)")* and *[Shameless](/wiki/Shameless_(UK_TV_series) "Shameless (UK TV series)")*. He co-created *[Cast Offs](/wiki/Cast_Offs "Cast Offs")*,[[20]](#cite_note-20) and co-wrote *[This Is England '86](/wiki/This_Is_England_%2786 "This Is England '86"),* *[This Is England '88](/wiki/This_Is_England_%2788 "This Is England '88")*, *[This Is England '90](/wiki/This_Is_England_%2790 "This Is England '90")*, and *[The Virtues](/wiki/The_Virtues_(TV_series) "The Virtues (TV series)")* with [Shane Meadows](/wiki/Shane_Meadows "Shane Meadows").[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Thorne was also in the running to write an episode for the [fifth series](/wiki/Doctor_Who_(series_5) "Doctor Who (series 5)") of *[Doctor Who](/wiki/Doctor_Who "Doctor Who")*, but amicably parted ways with the production.[[23]](#cite_note-23) In August 2010, [BBC Three](/wiki/BBC_Three "BBC Three") announced Thorne would be writing a 60-minute, six episode supernatural drama for the channel called *Touch*, later re-titled *[The Fades](/wiki/The_Fades_(TV_series) "The Fades (TV series)")*.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) In 2012, he won [BAFTA](/wiki/BAFTA "BAFTA") awards for both drama series (*[The Fades](/wiki/The_Fades_(TV_series) "The Fades (TV series)")*) and serial (*[This Is England '88](/wiki/This_Is_England_%2788 "This Is England '88")*).[[26]](#cite_note-26)[[27]](#cite_note-27) In 2014, Thorne's original rural teen murder drama *[Glue](/wiki/Glue_(TV_series) "Glue (TV series)")* premiered on [E4](/wiki/E4_(TV_channel) "E4 (TV channel)") and the show was nominated Best Multichannel Programme and the 2015 [Broadcast Awards](/wiki/Broadcast_Awards_(UK) "Broadcast Awards (UK)"). In autumn of 2015 *This Is England '90* was broadcast on Channel 4 and earned Thorne a Best Series Award at the Jameson Empire Awards 2016 and the BAFTA for Best Mini-Series in 2016. Next, the pan-European diamond-heist thriller for Sky Atlantic *[The Last Panthers](/wiki/The_Last_Panthers "The Last Panthers")*, which aired in the UK in September 2015, was BAFTA nominated for Best Drama Series. To round up a hat-trick of nominations at the 2016 BAFTA TV Awards, Thorne's BBC 3 drama *Don't Take My Baby* was nominated and went on to win the BAFTA for Best Single Drama. Thorne's Channel 4 drama *[National Treasure](/wiki/National_Treasure_(TV_drama) "National Treasure (TV drama)")* started on 20 September 2016 and won the BAFTA for Best Mini-Series in 2017.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In April 2016 it was announced that Thorne would be adapting [Philip Pullman](/wiki/Philip_Pullman "Philip Pullman")'s epic trilogy *[His Dark Materials](/wiki/His_Dark_Materials_(TV_series) "His Dark Materials (TV series)")* for BBC One.[[29]](#cite_note-29) In 2017, it was announced that he would write an episode of the [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4")/[Amazon Video](/wiki/Amazon_Video "Amazon Video") series *[Philip K. Dick's Electric Dreams](/wiki/Philip_K._Dick%27s_Electric_Dreams "Philip K. Dick's Electric Dreams")*[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31) and would write the [Damien Chazelle](/wiki/Damien_Chazelle "Damien Chazelle") [musical drama](/wiki/Musical_film "Musical film") [Netflix](/wiki/Netflix "Netflix") series *[The Eddy](/wiki/The_Eddy "The Eddy")*.[[32]](#cite_note-32) Thorne's four-part dark drama *[Kiri](/wiki/Kiri_(TV_series) "Kiri (TV series)")* began on Channel Four on 10 January 2018 and was nominated for Best Mini Series at the 2019 BAFTA's.[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-:5-34) His Channel Four show [*The Accident*](/wiki/The_Accident_(miniseries) "The Accident (miniseries)") began on 24 October 2019 and starred [Sarah Lancashire](/wiki/Sarah_Lancashire "Sarah Lancashire").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In 2021, Thorne wrote the television film *[Help](/wiki/Help_(2021_TV_film) "Help (2021 TV film)")*. Set and filmed in [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool "Liverpool"), *Help* focused on the plight of disabled people and their carers during the [COVID-19 pandemic in the UK](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_UK "COVID-19 pandemic in the UK") and addressed the multitude of ways in which [Boris Johnson's government](/wiki/Second_Johnson_ministry "Second Johnson ministry") had failed them. It was acclaimed by critics, with Carol Midgley of *[The Times](/wiki/The_Times "The Times")* calling it "a shaming nightmare [that] all ministers should see",[[36]](#cite_note-36) and won Best Drama at the 2021 Rose d'Or Awards.

In 2022, Thorne co-wrote *[Then Barbara Met Alan](/wiki/Then_Barbara_Met_Alan "Then Barbara Met Alan")* with [Genevieve Barr](/wiki/Genevieve_Barr "Genevieve Barr"), the true story of [Barbara Lisicki](/wiki/Barbara_Lisicki "Barbara Lisicki") and [Alan Holdsworth](/wiki/Alan_Holdsworth "Alan Holdsworth"), the founders of DAN ([Disabled People's Direct Action Network](/wiki/Disabled_People%27s_Direct_Action_Network "Disabled People's Direct Action Network")). It tells the story of two disabled cabaret performers who meet at a gig in 1989, fall in love and, driven by their own experiences and the experiences of those around them of discrimination, mistreatment, and the realities of living in an ableist society, lead protests nationwide, eventually leading to the passing of the [Disability Discrimination Act 1995](/wiki/Disability_Discrimination_Act_1995 "Disability Discrimination Act 1995"). *[Then Barbara Met Alan](/wiki/Then_Barbara_Met_Alan "Then Barbara Met Alan")* was received to both popular and critical acclaim, with Frances Ryan of [The Guardian](/wiki/The_Guardian "The Guardian") saying "By the time the real-life Barbara was on screen in the final scene – with a ramp symbolically coming out of a bus to finally give her entry – I was crying. For what we gained. For what was taken from us for decades, and still is. For the campaigners who gave so much for my generation and those that do today. Roar in the streets and kiss your lover. This is what disability looks like – and the battle continues."[[37]](#cite_note-37)

In 2023, Thorne wrote the four-part BBC drama *[Best Interests](/wiki/Best_Interests "Best Interests")*, starring [Michael Sheen](/wiki/Michael_Sheen "Michael Sheen") and [Sharon Horgan](/wiki/Sharon_Horgan "Sharon Horgan") as loving parents who are forced to make a harrowing decision about their daughter's medical care.

In 2025, several of Thorne’s television projects were released. These included the Netflix drama *[Toxic Town](/wiki/Toxic_Town "Toxic Town")*, which follows the story of three mothers involved in the Corby toxic waste case. The series was executive produced by [Charlie Brooker](/wiki/Charlie_Brooker "Charlie Brooker") and [Annabel Jones](/wiki/Annabel_Jones "Annabel Jones").[[38]](#cite_note-38) Netflix also released the four-part limited series *[Adolescence](/wiki/Adolescence_(TV_series) "Adolescence (TV series)")*, which Thorne co-wrote with [Stephen Graham](/wiki/Stephen_Graham "Stephen Graham") about a 13-year-old boy who is accused of murder. *[Adolescence](/wiki/Adolescence_(TV_series) "Adolescence (TV series)")* topped the UK’s weekly TV ratings, with its first episode pulling in 6.45 million viewers in its first week, according to ratings provider Barb.[[39]](#cite_note-39) It made history as the first streaming show to top the UK's weekly TV ratings.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Thorne also wrote about the [News International phone hacking scandal](/wiki/News_International_phone_hacking_scandal "News International phone hacking scandal") for the [ITVX](/wiki/ITVX "ITVX") television series *[The Hack](/wiki/The_Hack "The Hack")*[[41]](#cite_note-41) which was released in September 2025.

Filming began in 2025 of Thorne's romantic drama *[Falling](/wiki/Falling_(TV_series) "Falling (TV series)")*, starring [Keely Hawes](/wiki/Keely_Hawes "Keely Hawes") and [Paapa Essiedu](/wiki/Paapa_Essiedu "Paapa Essiedu").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Radio

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Radio")]

Thorne has written four plays for radio; an adaptation of *When You Cure Me* (BBC Radio 3, 2006[[44]](#cite_note-44)), *Left at the Angel* (BBC Radio 4, 2007[[45]](#cite_note-45)), an adaptation of *[The Hunchback of Notre-Dame](/wiki/The_Hunchback_of_Notre-Dame "The Hunchback of Notre-Dame")* (co-written with Alex Bulmer, BBC Radio 4, 2009[[46]](#cite_note-46)) and *People Snogging in Public Places* (BBC Radio 3, 2009[[47]](#cite_note-47)). The latter won him the [Sony Radio Academy Awards](/wiki/Sony_Radio_Academy_Awards "Sony Radio Academy Awards") Gold for Best Drama 2010.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The judges described it "as a wonderfully written and performed, highly original piece of radio drama in which the production perfectly mirrored the subject. Painful and funny, it was a bold exciting listen."[[49]](#cite_note-49) *A Summer Night* (BBC Radio 3, 2011) was Thorne's response to the 2011 London riots, transmitted live as part of the *Free Thinking* festival.

In 2012, *People Snogging in Public Places* was produced and broadcast by France-Culture (in the Fictions / Drôles de drames slot) under the French title of *Regarder passer les trains* (translator: Jacqueline Chnéour).

### Film

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Film")]

Thorne's first film *[The Scouting Book for Boys](/wiki/The_Scouting_Book_for_Boys "The Scouting Book for Boys")*[[50]](#cite_note-50) was released in 2009, it won him Best Newcomer at the [London Film Festival](/wiki/London_Film_Festival "London Film Festival").[[51]](#cite_note-51) The jury said, "Jack Thorne is a poetic writer with an end-of-the-world imagination and a real gift for story-telling."[[52]](#cite_note-52) Thorne has been commissioned to write feature films for producers both sides of the Atlantic, with credits including *[War Book](/wiki/War_Book "War Book")* starring [Sophie Okonedo](/wiki/Sophie_Okonedo "Sophie Okonedo") which [Tom Harper](/wiki/Tom_Harper_(director) "Tom Harper (director)") directed, and *[A Long Way Down](/wiki/A_Long_Way_Down_(film) "A Long Way Down (film)")* starring [Pierce Brosnan](/wiki/Pierce_Brosnan "Pierce Brosnan"), [Toni Collette](/wiki/Toni_Collette "Toni Collette") and [Aaron Paul](/wiki/Aaron_Paul "Aaron Paul") (directed by [Pascal Chaumeil](/wiki/Pascal_Chaumeil "Pascal Chaumeil")) based on the novel by [Nick Hornby](/wiki/Nick_Hornby "Nick Hornby").

On 8 May 2013, Thorne was hired to write *[Wonder](/wiki/Wonder_(film) "Wonder (film)")*, a film adaptation of the 2012 [novel of the same name](/wiki/Wonder_(Palacio_novel) "Wonder (Palacio novel)") by R.J. Palacio. Thorne co-wrote the script with [Steve Conrad](/wiki/Steve_Conrad "Steve Conrad") and [Stephen Chbosky](/wiki/Stephen_Chbosky "Stephen Chbosky"). The latter directed the film, which starred [Julia Roberts](/wiki/Julia_Roberts "Julia Roberts"), [Owen Wilson](/wiki/Owen_Wilson "Owen Wilson"), and [Jacob Tremblay](/wiki/Jacob_Tremblay "Jacob Tremblay") and was released on 17 November 2017.[[53]](#cite_note-53) On 2 August 2017, it was announced he would rewrite the script for *[Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker](/wiki/Star_Wars:_The_Rise_of_Skywalker "Star Wars: The Rise of Skywalker")*,[[54]](#cite_note-54) but on 12 September 2017, he was replaced by [J. J. Abrams](/wiki/J._J._Abrams "J. J. Abrams") and [Chris Terrio](/wiki/Chris_Terrio "Chris Terrio").[[55]](#cite_note-Abrams-55)[[56]](#cite_note-J._J._Abrams-56)[[57]](#cite_note-Replace-57) In 2018, it was announced that he would rewrite the initial screenplay penned by [Chris Weitz](/wiki/Chris_Weitz "Chris Weitz") for Disney's [live-action adaptation](/wiki/Pinocchio_(2022_live-action_film) "Pinocchio (2022 live-action film)") of *[Pinocchio](/wiki/Pinocchio_(1940_film) "Pinocchio (1940 film)")*, then to be directed by [Paul King](/wiki/Paul_King_(director) "Paul King (director)").[[58]](#cite_note-58)

Thorne also co-wrote the 2019 film *[The Aeronauts](/wiki/The_Aeronauts_(film) "The Aeronauts (film)")* with [Tom Harper](/wiki/Tom_Harper_(director) "Tom Harper (director)") for [Amazon Studios](/wiki/Amazon_Studios "Amazon Studios"), starring [Felicity Jones](/wiki/Felicity_Jones "Felicity Jones") and [Eddie Redmayne](/wiki/Eddie_Redmayne "Eddie Redmayne"). Although Amazon does not release exact streaming figures, Jennifer Salke, Head of Amazon Studios said in an interview with *Deadline Hollywood* that as of January 2020 *The Aeronauts* was the most viewed movie of all time on Amazon Prime.[[59]](#cite_note-59)

2020 saw the release of three more films written by Thorne, including [*Radioactive*](/wiki/Radioactive_(film) "Radioactive (film)"), a biographical drama about [Marie Curie](/wiki/Marie_Curie "Marie Curie"), starring [Rosamund Pike](/wiki/Rosamund_Pike "Rosamund Pike"); *[The Secret Garden](/wiki/The_Secret_Garden_(2020_film) "The Secret Garden (2020 film)"),* an adaptation of the novel of the same name; and *[Enola Holmes](/wiki/Enola_Holmes_(film) "Enola Holmes (film)")*, about the sister of Sherlock Holmes, starring [Millie Bobby Brown](/wiki/Millie_Bobby_Brown "Millie Bobby Brown") and [Helena Bonham Carter](/wiki/Helena_Bonham_Carter "Helena Bonham Carter"). Thorne would also write [the sequel](/wiki/Enola_Holmes_2 "Enola Holmes 2"), which released on Netflix in 2022.[[60]](#cite_note-Ferme-60)

Thorne co-wrote the film *[The Swimmers](/wiki/The_Swimmers_(2022_film) "The Swimmers (2022 film)")* in 2022, which follows two young sisters on their harrowing journey as refugees, from war-torn Syria to the 2016 Rio Olympics. In 2024, he co-wrote *[Joy](/wiki/Joy_(2024_film) "Joy (2024 film)")*, about three pioneering British scientists in the '60s and '70s and their struggle to develop IVF — against all odds.

Thorne wrote a treatment to the 2025 sequel to *[Tron: Legacy](/wiki/Tron:_Legacy "Tron: Legacy")* (2010), titled *[Tron: Ares](/wiki/Tron:_Ares "Tron: Ares")*, though he ultimately went uncredited.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

## Campaigning and advocacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Campaigning and advocacy")]

Thorne has been a long-term advocate for the disabled community in the dramatic arts. After he developed [cholinergic urticaria](/wiki/Cholinergic_urticaria "Cholinergic urticaria") when he was 20 years old, he became allergic to outdoor heat, artificial heat, and his own body heat. This gave him chronic pain that forced him to leave university and spend much of his early twenties in bed.[[62]](#cite_note-youtube.com-62) Despite this, he felt unsure whether he could identify as a disabled person; after attending a [Graeae Theatre Company](/wiki/Graeae_Theatre_Company "Graeae Theatre Company") open day (which he described as the "National Theatre of disability") three years after his diagnosis, he was accepted with open arms. He described the incident as a "[coming out](/wiki/Coming_out "Coming out") moment" and a "crucial part" of who he is. He has since written disabled dramas *The Hunchback of Notre Dame*, *The Spastic King*, *[Cast Offs](/wiki/Cast_Offs "Cast Offs")*, *The Solid Life of Sugar Water*, *Don't Take My Baby*, *[CripTales](/wiki/Criptales "Criptales")*, and *[Then Barbara Met Alan](/wiki/Then_Barbara_Met_Alan "Then Barbara Met Alan")*, and has become a patron of the Graeae Theatre Company.

In August 2021, Thorne delivered the [Edinburgh TV Festival](/wiki/Edinburgh_TV_Festival "Edinburgh TV Festival")'s prestigious [MacTaggart Lecture](/wiki/MacTaggart_Lecture "MacTaggart Lecture").[[62]](#cite_note-youtube.com-62) He used the speech to discuss television's power as an "empathy box" in the living room of millions and its failings for neglecting a large and vibrant part of the populace by poorly representing the disabled community. Thorne points to the great suffering of disabled people during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic") in which the media rendered huge numbers of deaths acceptable through usage of the term "underlying health condition".[[62]](#cite_note-youtube.com-62) The speech also outlined how television industry practice has been discriminatory towards disabled artists, and the dire need for the industry to commit to change, both off-screen and on; alongside [Genevieve Barr](/wiki/Genevieve_Barr "Genevieve Barr") and Katie Player, Thorne announced a pressure group called Underlying Health Condition which aims to elevate disabled voices in the industry. Thorne argues that more disabled stories written by disabled people and performed by disabled people would make visible what's invisible in the "empathy box" in the homes of the public and cause change to happen.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

On 3 December 2021, [Underlying Health Condition](/wiki/Underlying_Health_Condition "Underlying Health Condition") was launched at an event at the [Tate Modern](/wiki/Tate_Modern "Tate Modern"), collaborating with other disability organisations such as Disabled Artists Networking Community, the Creative Diversity Network and 1in4 Coalition, to propose a series of requirements and measures to accommodate and support disabled artists in television.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-64) This, in turn, led to the launch of The TV Access Project,[[65]](#cite_note-65) or TAP, which has seen 10 of the UK's biggest broadcasters commit to the full inclusion of Deaf Disabled and/or Neurodivergent Talent by 2030. TAP created best practice guidelines to ensure this inclusion, referred to as the 5As: Anticipate, Ask, Assess, Adjust, Advocate.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Within its first year, TAP delivered 20 sustainable, tangible solutions towards its vision of full inclusion, including launching TAPStars, a programme funded by the broadcasters and streamers who are TAP members to support early-career disabled off-screen talent; introducing an Access To Work pilot scheme to fast-track applications and access provision for freelances, and oversee reimbursements from Access to Work; securing a commitment from all TAP members to fund necessary access costs not covered by Access to Work, over and above the production budget; giving 82 Commissioning Editors and senior leaders from TAP members fundamental 5As training, delivered by CDN[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]; and writing an outline of key access-related roles and responsibilities across productions and commissioning, to be adopted alongside access coordinators.[[67]](#cite_note-67)

In August 2024, it was announced that all 10 TAP members - the [BBC](/wiki/BBC "BBC"), [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4"), [ITV](/wiki/ITV_(TV_network) "ITV (TV network)"), [Disney+](/wiki/Disney%2B "Disney+"), [UKTV](/wiki/UKTV "UKTV"), [BritBox](/wiki/BritBox "BritBox"), [Sky UK](/wiki/Sky_UK "Sky UK"), [Paramount Pictures](/wiki/Paramount_Pictures "Paramount Pictures"), [STV](/wiki/STV_(TV_channel) "STV (TV channel)") and [Amazon Prime Video](/wiki/Amazon_Prime_Video "Amazon Prime Video") – would boycott any studios and production spaces which had not completed the TV Access Project's accessibility audit by August 2025.[[68]](#cite_note-68) This audit requires studios to self-assess their own accessibility in 4 key areas: Production buildings, Locations – external, Locations – Internal, and Outside Broadcast.

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Jack_Thorne&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Thorne is married to Rachel Mason. They underwent multiple attempts at IVF to have their son, Elliott. The experience later led them to develop the film [*Joy*](/wiki/Joy_(2024_film) "Joy (2024 film)").[[69]](#cite_note-69) His wife's sister Cath is married to the comedian [Frank Skinner](/wiki/Frank_Skinner "Frank Skinner").[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In 2022, Thorne was diagnosed as being [autistic](/wiki/Autism "Autism"). He was inspired to seek diagnosis following a question on *[Desert Island Discs](/wiki/Desert_Island_Discs "Desert Island Discs")*.[[71]](#cite_note-71)
